summary: Gruber dissects MSNBC's rebranding to 'MS NOW' as it spins out of NBCUniversal into the new Versant media company. He critiques the forced backronym ('My Source for News, Opinion and the World') as needlessly dumb, notes the confusion over whether 'NOW' is pronounced as a word or spelled out, and savages the new logo as looking like an Austrian flag or a receipt printer's end-of-roll warning. He points out the irony that CNBC, also spinning out, gets to keep its name unchanged. He offers his own alternative name suggestion (MNC) and provides historical context on how the original MSNBC name lost its Microsoft connection two decades ago.
key_points: The 'My Source for News, Opinion and the World' backronym is gratuitous — acronyms like CNN work fine without standing for anything meaningful
The MS NOW logo is atrocious, resembling an Austrian flag or a receipt printer warning rather than an American news brand
CNBC gets to keep its name in the same Versant spinout because its 'NBC' technically stands for something different, highlighting the arbitrariness of the rebranding
Microsoft hasn't been involved with MSNBC since 2005, so the 'MS' has been meaningless for 20 years — no backronym was needed
The parent company name 'Versant' itself feels like a fake company from a movie, setting the tone for the botched subsidiary rebrandings
money_quotes: The 'My Source News Opinion World' backronym is so dumb it boggles the mind. I genuinely wonder if someone had ChatGPT do that.
You can have a series of letters as a name — especially as a TV channel — without those letters really standing for anything.
Nobody has ever thought about what the old acronym stood for and nobody needed a new fake one.
My personal benchmark for a logo is that it shouldn't look like a pension fund.
What is that flag? It looks like the Austrian flag, not America's. But are we sure it even is a flag?
Versant itself is a pretty bad name — feels so vague — seems like the name of a fake company in a movie or TV show.
key_insight: When rebranding is forced by corporate restructuring rather than driven by creative vision, the result is predictably awful — unnecessary backronyms, confused pronunciation, and logos that look like pension funds.
